children.value: RNNs are natural models for sequential data, but the

children.html: O(T)\cO(T)O(T)

children.value: time complexity of evaluation and backpropagating gradients is a severe limitation. In modern machine learning, one of the deciding factors is how many training epochs you can perform for a fixed computational budget. To that end, architectures that can process an entire sequence in parallel are advantageous. Transformers are one such architecture.

Transformers underlie large language models (LLMs) like Open AI’s ChatGPT and Google’s Gemini. They are also widely used in computer vision and other domains of machine learning. This chapter walks through the basic building blocks of a transformer: self-attention, token-wise nonlinear transformations, layer norm, and positional encodings. We follow the presentation of
with some modifications for consistency with these notes.
Before a transformer can process text, the raw string must be converted into a sequence of discrete

children.html: V\cVV

children.value: . The choice of tokenization scheme involves a tradeoff: word-level tokenization yields short sequences but requires a very large vocabulary and cannot handle unknown words; character-level tokenization handles any input but produces very long sequences that stress the

children.html: O(T2)O(T^2)O(T2)

children.value: , which decomposes text into vocabulary units that are between characters and words in granularity. The dominant algorithm is
, which starts from a character vocabulary and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair of symbols until the vocabulary reaches a target size

children.html: ∣V∣|\cV|∣V∣

children.value: (typically 32k–128k tokens). Common words become single tokens; rare words are split into subword pieces. Because BPE operates on bytes, it handles any Unicode text without unknown-token issues.

The output of the transformer will be another matrix of the same shape,

children.html: X(M)∈RT×D\mbX^{(M)} \in \reals^{T \times D}X(M)∈RT×D

children.value: . These output features can be used for downstream tasks like sentiment classification, machine translation, or autoregressive modeling.
The output results from a stack of transformer blocks,

children.html: X(m)=transformer-block(X(m−1)).\mbX^{(m)} = \texttt{transformer-block}(\mbX^{(m-1)}).X(m)=transformer-block(X(m−1)).

children.value: Each block consists of two stages: one that operates vertically, combining information across the sequence length; another that operates horizontally, combining information across feature dimensions.
The first stage combines information across sequence length using a mechanism called
. Mathematically, attention is a weighted average,

children.value: Where does the attention matrix come from? In a transformer, the attention weights are determined by the pairwise similarity of tokens in the sequence. The simplest instantiation would be

children.html: At,s=exp⁡{xt⊤xs}∑s′=1Texp⁡{xt⊤xs′}.A_{t,s} = \frac{\exp \{ \mbx_t^\top \mbx_s\}}{\sum_{s'=1}^T \exp\{\mbx_t^\top \mbx_{s'}\}}.At,s​=∑s′=1T​exp{xt⊤​xs′​}exp{xt⊤​xs​}​.

children.value: Rather than propagating information about past tokens via a hidden state, a transformer with causal attention can directly attend to any past token.

and retrieves them when generating each new token. This reduces the per-step cost from

The residual connections in a transformer — introduced as an optimization technique in
— have a deeper architectural interpretation. Writing out the full forward pass,

children.html: xt(M)=xt(0)+∑m=1M[mhsa(m)(X(m−1))t+mlp(m)(yt(m))],\mbx_t^{(M)} = \mbx_t^{(0)} + \sum_{m=1}^{M} \left[ \texttt{mhsa}^{(m)}(\mbX^{(m-1)})_t + \texttt{mlp}^{(m)}(\mby_t^{(m)}) \right],xt(M)​=xt(0)​+m=1∑M​[mhsa(m)(X(m−1))t​+mlp(m)(yt(m)​)],

children.value: reveals that every component — every attention head and every MLP — adds its output directly onto a shared
that begins as the token embedding and accumulates updates across all layers
All components read from and write to the same space.
Attention heads and MLPs interact not through layer boundaries but through their shared updates to the stream. One head can write information that a later head in a different layer reads directly.
Each head is a low-rank read-write.
With the Q/K/V/O factorization, each attention head reads from the stream via

children.html: Uq\mbU_qUq​
Uk\mbU_kUk​

children.value: , computes an update, and writes it back via

children.html: UoUv\mbU_o \mbU_vUo​Uv​
KKK
DDD

children.value: The residual stream framing is the foundation of mechanistic interpretability: by analyzing which subspaces different heads and MLPs read from and write to, researchers have identified circuits that implement specific algorithms (induction, copying, retrieval) inside trained transformers.

After the multi-headed self-attention step, the transformer applies a token-wise nonlinear transformation to mix feature dimensions. This is done with a feedforward network applied identically at each position,

children.html: xt(m)=mlp(yt(m)).\mbx_t^{(m)} = \texttt{mlp}(\mby_t^{(m)}).xt(m)​=mlp(yt(m)​).

children.value: The MLP typically has hidden dimension

children.html: 4D4D4D

children.value: , so the computational complexity of this step is

children.html: O(TD2)\cO(TD^2)O(TD2)

children.value: . For transformers with very large feature dimensions, this is the dominant cost.

LayerNorm stabilizes training by z-scoring each token and applying a learned shift and scale:

children.html: layer-norm(xt)=β+γ⊙(xt−mean(xt)std(xt)),\texttt{layer-norm}(\mbx_t) = \mbbeta + \mbgamma \odot \left( \frac{\mbx_t - \texttt{mean}(\mbx_t)}{\texttt{std}(\mbx_t)} \right),layer-norm(xt​)=β+γ⊙(std(xt​)xt​−mean(xt​)​),
β,γ∈RD\mbbeta, \mbgamma \in \reals^Dβ,γ∈RD

children.value: are learned parameters. LayerNorm is applied before each sub-layer (Pre-LN), which yields more stable training than the original Post-LN design:

children.html: Y(m)=X(m−1)+mhsa(layer-norm(X(m−1)))X(m)=Y(m)+mlp(layer-norm(Y(m))).\begin{aligned} \mbY^{(m)} &= \mbX^{(m-1)} + \texttt{mhsa}(\texttt{layer-norm}(\mbX^{(m-1)})) \\ \mbX^{(m)} &= \mbY^{(m)} + \texttt{mlp}(\texttt{layer-norm}(\mbY^{(m)})). \end{aligned}Y(m)X(m)​=X(m−1)+mhsa(layer-norm(X(m−1)))=Y(m)+mlp(layer-norm(Y(m))).​

Without explicit position information, a transformer treats its inputs as an
of tokens — the architecture is permutation-equivariant (subject only to the causal mask). When the data have spatial or temporal structure, position must be injected explicitly.
The original transformer adds a fixed position vector to each token embedding:

children.html: xt(0)=ct+pt,\mbx_t^{(0)} = \mbc_t + \mbp_t,xt(0)​=ct​+pt​,
ct∈RD\mbc_t \in \reals^Dct​∈RD
pt∈RD\mbp_t \in \reals^Dpt​∈RD

children.value: encodes the position using sinusoidal basis functions
. Learned absolute position embeddings are also common.
Absolute encodings bake position into the token embedding before the attention computation, which limits the model’s ability to generalize to sequence lengths unseen during training.
instead encode position directly into the query–key dot product by rotating query and key vectors before the inner product.

children.value: . This relative-position property makes RoPE extrapolate more gracefully to longer sequences than absolute encodings. RoPE is now the dominant positional encoding in open-weight LLMs (LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma).

children.html: ttt

children.value: Standard practice is to use the AdamW optimizer with gradient clipping, a warmup-then-cosine learning rate schedule, and dropout. Treat these as hyperparameters; the optimal settings are model- and data-dependent.

The transformer landscape is evolving too rapidly for any static summary to remain useful for long. That said, students interested in working with LLMs directly have access to a growing ecosystem of high-quality open-weight models — models whose weights are publicly released even if training details are not always fully disclosed. Current families worth being aware of include Meta’s

series. These span a range of sizes (1B–405B parameters) and are available through Hugging Face, Ollama, and similar platforms. All of them implement the core building blocks described in this chapter — Pre-LN, RoPE, GQA, and SwiGLU — with the main differences lying in scale, training data, and fine-tuning for instruction following.

Transformers achieve parallelism over a full sequence by replacing the sequential hidden-state recursion with multi-headed self-attention: each token directly attends to all previous tokens, enabling gradient information to flow in a single step rather than through

children.html: TTT

children.value: chained Jacobians. Viewing the architecture through the lens of the residual stream clarifies that attention heads and MLPs all operate on a shared representational space, with each component contributing a low-rank additive update. The quadratic

children.html: O(T2)O(T^2)O(T2)

children.value: cost of softmax attention is a practical bottleneck for long sequences and has motivated substantial recent work on more efficient alternatives.
— replaces softmax attention with linear-time approximations and connects transformers back to structured state space models
, “Attention is All You Need.” The original transformer paper.
, “An Introduction to Transformers.” Accessible overview following a similar presentation to this chapter.
, “A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits.” Introduces the residual stream perspective and the circuit-level analysis of transformer computation.
, “RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding.”
